original_abstract: Readily available $o$-vinylphenols undergo a formal (5 + 2) cycloaddition with alkynes in the presence of catalytic [Cp*RhCl$_2$]$_2$ and Cu(OAc)$_2$. This transformation involves the selective cleavage of the terminal C–H bond of the alkenyl moiety, providing a practical, versatile, and atom-economical route to highly valuable benzoxepine skeletons. Furthermore, substituting the alkyne with carbon monoxide as the reaction partner enables a formal (5 + 1) cycloaddition, yielding coumarin products. This methodology represents a rare example of using C–H functionalization to assemble seven-membered heterocycles from simple precursors.
humanized_abstract: Common $o$-vinylphenols give the formal (5 + 2) cycloaddition products with alkynes in the presence of catalytic amounts of [Cp*RhCl2]2 and Cu(OAc)2; the terminal C–H of the alkenyl unit is selectively activated towards Rh and subsequently cleaved. The protocol uses readily available starting materials for the formal preparation of valuable heterocycles like benzoxepines in a highly efficient and atom-economical fashion. When the alkyne is substituted with CO, a formal (5 + 1) cycloaddition affords coumarin derivatives. The procedure is amongst the few instances of constructing 7-membered heterocycles by utilizing C–H bond functionalization in common chemistry and utilizing relatively simple starting materials.

The protocol uses readily available starting materials for the formal preparation of valuable heterocycles like benzoxepines in a highly efficient and atom-economical fashion. When the alkyne is substituted with CO, a formal (5 + 1) cycloaddition affords coumarin derivatives. The procedure is amongst the few instances of constructing 7-membered heterocycles by utilizing C–H bond functionalization in common chemistry and utilizing relatively simple starting materials.
